title: MARY MIDGLEY By Clare Mac Cumhaill
text:
[Music]
Hello, my name is Clara Mcool and I work
in Durham University in the UK in the
philosophy department. In this video,
I'm going to tell you about the
pioneering British moral philosopher
Mary Midgley. Mitchley died in 2018 at
the age of 99. She wrote 18 or 19 books
depending on how you count with her
first book, Beast and Man, the roots of
human nature, written when she was 59.
Inspiration for those of us who may be
late comers to philosophy or who have
philosophical ideas brewing. As I'll
explain in this video, much of Midley's
later work springs from Beast and Man,
including her influential idea that
philosophy is necessary to human life.
Mitchley was born Mary Scrutton in 1919.
Shortly after her birth, her father, Tom
Scrutton, became chaplain at King's
College Chapel in Cambridge. The family
didn't stay long in Cambridge. They
moved to a rectory in Greenford, now in
Greater London, when Miji was five. In
her memoir, which I recommend, The Owl
of Manurva, Miji says that the real
business of her early life began with
her discovery of its garden. That was
where she first experienced in herself
the deep biophilia or love of life and
living things that would resurface in
her early midlife in her 30s and come to
shape her philosophical thinking. Miji
came from a family of educated women.
Her father's sister Jane had studied
history at Girtton College in Cambridge
and three of her mother's aunts had
qualified to teach in the 1880s. This
included her aunt Jeanie, whose copy of
Darwin's 1872, the expression of the
emotions in man and animals was given to
Mary after Jeanie's death. Miji's
secondary school was down house, the
country home of Charles Darwin, where
that very book had been
composed. Miji went to Oxford in n 1938
to study mods and great, five terms of
classics followed by seven terms of
philosophy. She'd been in Vienna earlier
that year to learn German and had seen
Nazi tanks rolling into the center of
the city. Later, her vianese host fled
to England and took refuge in her
parents' house. On her first day at
Oxford, Miji met Iris Murdoch, the
philosopher and novelist. Both were at
Somerville College and the pair became
lifelong friends. Their tutor was Donald
McKinnon, later a professor of theology
at Aberdine and Cambridge. He was a
conscientious objector during the war
and he conveyed to all his students the
seriousness of
philosophy. Another of his duties was
Philip a foot a year below Mary and Iris
at Somerville. She too went on to become
a distinguished moral philosopher.
Elizabeth Anskam the towering
metaphysician of action and mind and
translator of Vickenstein's
philosophical investigations was a year
above at St. Hughes. She would
occasionally visit Somerville, which is
where Miji recalls first meeting her in
the dining hall. She pressed Midgley on
the nature of Plato's forms. Miji
remembers saying to herself, "That woman
is worth
cultivating." The war and its aftermath
is the inevitable backdrop to the
philosophy that each of these thinkers
went on to develop. In various writings,
Miji has attributed their distinctive
philosophical orientation to the
education they received during the war
when numbers of male students dropped.
Many of their lecturers were Jewish
refugee scholars who had fled Nazi
Germany and they were often taught in
seminars, a German style of class that
was unlike the traditional Oxford
tutorial. She has said that in those
wartime classes which were small,
students were more interested in
understanding this deeply puzzling world
than in putting each other down. Here
referring to a way of doing philosophy
that encourages, in her words, a lot of
clever young men to compete in winning
arguments.
In general, it might be said that her
philosophy aims at synthesis, an
approach that resonates with that of RG
Collingwood, the Wayne Fleet professor
of philosophy and father of her friend
Ruth. She attended some of the last
lectures he gave before his death in
1943. Collingwood distinguishes
aristical from dialectical methods in
philosophy. The aristical method
involves assuming that there are two
opposing positions and only one is right
or true. The dialectical model takes it
that apparently opposing positions both
have some truth in them and the task is
to view each one from a perspective that
can sift and incorporate what is true in
both. This requires understanding and
not
opposition. After graduation, Midley
undertook war work in the civil service
in the Ministry of Production and
briefly taught classics at a boy's
public school. She left on the day it
was announced the Labour Party had won a
large majority in the post-war election
and she returned to Oxford. She became
secretary to the illustrious classicist
Gilbert Murray, but she slowly found
herself being drawn back to philosophy
and more particularly to what she called
large-scale
metaphysics. In 1947, she started work
on a doctoral thesis on Plutinus'
psychology of the self. The ego is first
used as a philosophical term in
Platinus's Aniads, which perhaps goes
some way to explaining the frequent
mention of Freud and Young in Midgley's
later work. Like Plato, Plutinus
believed there was, and now I quote
Midgley, a far vaster and more serious
reality lying behind the everyday scene
around us. Unlike Plato, however,
Plutinus did not denigrate the sensory
or everyday world as mere appearance.
Instead, he wanted to find a wider, more
inclusive perspective that could show
the continuity of the physical and the
spiritual. Midley never finished her
thesis, but we are lucky that the
manuscript survives and is in the
archives here at Durham
University. Midgley was not religious,
but she she certainly regarded as
spiritual the need that humans have for
understanding. The attitudes that belong
to any attempt at understanding the
vastness of the truth are for her
religious attitudes. Though they can
they can become misdirected. We can
place our entire faith in science for
example. Ultimately they are
contemplative. They are reaching for
some glimpse of what is whole. In
Midley's nature in her mature
metaphysics it is this living planet
earth a self-regulating and sustaining
biosphere of which we are part that
should direct our attention be the
proper object of our awe our reverence
even our gratitude. Mary Scrutton
married in 1950 with Iris Murdoch as her
bridesmaid. I'm now going to tell you a
bit about her philosophical ideas
starting with some of her earliest
writing. By the time of her death, Miji
was one of the foremost public
philosophers in the UK. But even in the
40s and 50s, her abundant flare for
public philosophy was philosophy was
clear. She began writing radio scripts
for the BBC which were sub subsequently
printed in the listener magazine. Some
drew from her thesis on Platinus,
including the 1951 The Natural History
of Contradictions, the byline of which
reads Mary Scrutton on
paradoxy. In this article, we get a
taste for her sensitivity to conflict
and of course paradox and logical
inconsistency and a sheer ubiquity in
human life and thought. We see here her
early and sharp departure from a style
of linguistic philosophy that is ruled
by the Vikingsian slogan. Everything
that can be said can be said clearly.
Far from it, she said. There are many,
many human truths that cannot be told
without paradox or metaphor or without
wit or poetry. This is particularly true
of the practical reformer who may even
be a philosopher. Man is free but is
everywhere in chain. Not all her radio
scripts were welcomed. Sometime in the
early 1950s, Midgley wrote a piece
called Rings and Books, which listed all
those great canonical philosophers who
were bachelors. The list is long, and
she wrote, quote, I do not cram the
groaning scale with monks and friars
because there's always the chance that
they had some other reason besides
philosophy for joining their orders. On
the married list, only Socrates,
Aristotle, and Hegel are named. She
admits she may be wrong in the details,
but the figures are significant. Not
only is our inherited philosophical
tradition shaped entirely by men on her
reckoning, but mostly by bachelors. She
goes on to query, why is it that we are
stunned by Daycart's discovery of the
cogito? Her diagnosis, it appeals to the
adolescent philosopher in all of us.
Daycart meditating in his dressing gown
alone by his stove is not unlike an
overly intense teenager holed up in his
or her bedroom. She thinks mature people
living a normal domestic life could not
regard the existence of other people as
something that must be
inferred. The script was rejected by the
editor with a cursory assessment. A
trivial, irrelevant intrusion of
domestic matters into intellectual life.
But of course, Midi was trying to
contest what intellectual life itself
consists in and where it lies. Let me
quote from rings and books so you can
get a flavor of her
protest. Philosophers have generally
talked as though it were obvious that
one consciousness went to one body, as
though each person were a closed system
which could only signal to another by
external behavior and that behavior had
to be interpreted from previous
experience. I wonder whether they would
have said the same if they had been
frequently pregnant and suckling. If
they had been constantly faced with
questions like, "What have you been
eating to make him ill?
that the lived bodily experience of a
woman can be a source of knowledge and
philosophical insight explains her
sweeping conclusion. She writes, "An
account of human knowledge which women's
whole experience falsifies is inadequate
and partial and
capricious. The married Aristotle fares
slightly better in Midgley's estimation.
His ideas, she thinks, do have all the
free movement of maturity. Despite his
general assumption that men's function
and virtue is greater than women's, his
attempt at understanding was not so
partial as to cut off as irrelevant the
experience of half the
species. Miji and her husband, the
philosopher Jeff Miggley, had three
children together. After their marriage,
they moved to Newcastle in the northeast
of England, where Jeff had a position at
Newcastle University.
Miji had given up a teaching position at
the University of Reading in order to
join him and was not immediately anxious
to return to the lecture podium.
Instead, she wrote book reviews for the
New Statesman while continuing to write
articles for established philosophical
journals. By then, Midgley was already
on the philosophical path that would
lead to her mature philosophy. In her
30s, she read Conrad Lorenza's King
Solomon's Ring, a canonical work of
ethology or the study of free roaming
animals in their natural habitat. She
recovered the biophilia of her early
youth in Greenford and began, in her
words, raiding the libraries of
Newcastle so as to read as much as she
could on the subject of ethology. She
was preparing to write a work of moral
philosophy in which the study of gulls,
elephants, bees, wolves, and apes would
show us something about ourselves. Beast
and man, the roots of human nature was
published in 1978.
Oxford, Miji said, had caused her pen to
freeze. At the time, Oxford moral
philosophers were concerned to give an
analysis of the logic of statements like
stealing is wrong or you ought to keep
your promises. Before the war, a young
AJ had published language, truth, and
logic. The aim of that book was to root
out any kind of statement the meaning of
which could not be analyzed in terms of
possible experiences. That is to say, in
terms of experiences that could provide
a verification of what the sentence was
purporting to say. Remember the slogan,
anything that can be said can be said
clearly. Metaphysical and religious
statements did not seem to be verifiable
in this sense and so heir dismissed them
as nonsense. Ethical statements like
stealing is wrong were not verifiable
were not verifiable either. What kind of
experience could verify the truth of the
sentence stealing is wrong? Air thereby
offered another analysis. When we say
something is wrong, we are expressing
our disapproval or dislike of that
thing. In this sense, is wrong is a bit
like saying boo to
stealing. He's position became known as
emotivism. What it amounted to was an
attack on the objectivity of morality.
There is no act or happening or state of
affairs that is right and wrong in
itself. It just depends on how we feel
about it. The same goes for sentences
using moral obligation terms like ought.
There is nothing as such that we ought
to do. Something that the prescriptivist
RM Hair a contemporary of Miji's in um
insisted in his early work the language
of morals. Rather it depends on what
principles we or the societies we live
in have chosen to adopt. And if we don't
act in light of the principles that we
have freely chosen, we are behaving
irrationally either by our own standards
or by those of our
society. Midley rejected this p picture
in beast and man effectively saying hold
on and now I am quoting her again. She
writes, "The evils in the world in this
world are real. That they are so is
neither a fancy imposed on us by our
culture nor one created by our will and
imposed on the world." She described
heir's work as moral weed killer and the
moral the moral philosophy seen in
Oxford at the time as an arid garden.
Philosophers appeared to be concerned
only with terms like good and ought and
not with exploring the rich moral
vocabulary which we so patently have. In
moving to Newcastle, she had quietly
escaped that arid garden. She had
slipped over the wall, in her words, and
into the
jungle. The moral philosophers who were
busy spreading weed killer in their arid
gardens accepted a dichotomy which Miji
did not. They assumed that a complete
factual description of how things are in
the physical and material world would
not require the use of value terms. Such
terms are instead expressive of our
attitudes and preferences. For Midgley,
the ready acceptance of this dichotomy
belongs to a dualistic postcarteesian
metaphysics that separ that separates
lifeless matter, the body, from
mind. Following the married Aristotle,
the moral philosophy that she develops
in beast and man begins instead with the
assumption that there are living things,
creatures, each with their own modes or
ways of living, patterns that the
ethologist describes.
Living creatures have characteristic
needs and what they value is connected
to what fulfills what fulfills those
needs. Moreover, living creatures move
purposefully in the world and in a
structured intelligible way. This is
because they're motivated to go after
what they need. On this understanding,
what is valued is just what is just what
is needed by a particular kind of
creature. A living creature who is not a
fantasm or a ghost or a machine, but a
flesh and blood furred or feathered or
scaled animal who has evolved to live in
a particular way in a particular kind of
ecology amongst other creatures of its
kind and amongst a multitude of diverse
kinds of living organisms, small, even
microscopic and large. Seen from the
perspective of the planetary hole, all
these forms of life are interconnected.
They are
interdependent. In the human case, our
needs are of course many, which is why
the kinds of things we value are so many
and so varied. We need attachment and
intimacy, which is why we value
friendship and fellowship. We have
aesthetic needs. We have a need for
stimulation and independence, a need to
play and create and explore, a need even
for philosophy, something which I'm
going to return to. We have a need to
belong and to feel secure. We need
parental affection and recognition. We
need rest. This is all part of our human
nature, and the list goes on and on. As
social animals, many of the goods that
are related to our needs are connected
to our sociality and can only be
satisfied in community with others and
through the cultures and institutions
that according to Miji we create to
satisfy our natural passion of
motives. It is against this background,
this image of our nature that Midgley's
distinctive moral philosophy, a form of
ethical naturalism comes into view.
Because we value many things and have
many needs, conflict inevitably arises.
We can't have or do everything. We only
have one life to live and it is a life
lived with others, including with other
animals. In her book, Animals and Why
They Matter, she points out that humans
have always lived with other creatures
in what she calls mixed communities. But
because we only have one life to lead,
certain values must take priority and
shape our modes of interaction. and
ultimately the path through space and
time that the unfolding life of each
individual takes. The central business
of morality midley thinks is to compare
and relate the goods we go after the
values and to ensure that our motives
are seek our motives in seeking out such
goods remain balanced and in check. For
instance, the need for security which is
a good can be satisfied in different
ways depending on how we are motivated
to go after that good. are going after
security may for example result in
dominance especially where the instinct
towards possessiveness is strong. It may
issue in meanness or in an overthrifty
anxiety about the future or it may be
fulfilled through an openness to
fellowship or by habitual contribution
to communal projects that may foster a
sense of belonging and hence stability.
It is striking that for midi hardly any
motivation to seek out something that is
of value to us being the kind of
creatures we are is worthy or vicious in
itself except perhaps the motivation to
destroy which is something she discusses
in her 1984 book
wickedness. What matters rather is
proportionality that one motive does not
c crowd out the others and dull our
capacity to live as we are capable of
living. Reason is the name of the
faculty that plays this balancing and
ordering role. But feeling or emotion is
integral and indeed for her the pair are
really one. This allows her to develop a
distinctive account of the reality of
evil. We might put it this way. Evil is
a formal feature that belongs to the way
in which certain human motives are
ordered in the life of an individual,
group, institution or society.
Crucially, Midgley believes that, and
I'm quoting her here, because society is
the condition of man's living at all,
and because there is some evil, namely
friction, in any society, evil too is in
some sense natural to him. He has, like
any other species, his own natural
evils.
In developing this account, she insists
that ethology has an especially
important contribution to make to moral
philosophy. By seeing our continuity
with other kinds of living creatures in
so far as we may value the same things
or display similar motivational
patterns, ethology enables us to ask
questions about ourselves that our
culture can sometimes stop us from
asking. This is because certain
questions may lead us to uncover
motivational patterns that we might wish
to ignore or suppress. And this is
precisely where she thinks Freud's work
has proved enormously useful, if
reductive. It's reductive because for
him, roughly speaking, it's all about
sex. But for Midgley, though sex is
important, it is not the most
fundamental motivation in human life any
more than the motivation to compete or
win or survive is. In fact, since we are
social animals and since many human
goods are both realized in and
constituted by society, the willingness
to cooperate and act with others to
secure, share in or create those goods
is for midi a central human motivation.
We are genial
creatures. It is in this context that
the concept of selfishness is
intelligible. It describes an individual
who is not so genial, so motivated to
share and cooperate. On this
understanding, genes cannot be said to
be selfish as the neodyarwinian theorist
Richard Dawkins has argued or urged. The
notion of a selfish gene is a misnomer.
Still, Midley recognized that the idea
had a kind of power, including a kind of
political power that she saw it was
important to diffuse, and she became one
of Dawkins's fiercest and most
relentless public critics.
It is difficult to emphasize how
thoroughly unlike any other moral
philosophy that was written at the time
beast and man was and still
is. So far I have painted a picture of
Midgley's metaphysics in broad strokes.
Reality is vast and understanding
involves relating diverse and partial
perspectives a form of synthesis. Humans
are animals whose nature is continuous
with other living creatures of diverse
kinds and is characterized by a
distinctive structure of motives which
reason and feeling can order in the life
of an individual depending on what is
valued which is also to say
needed with this thumbnail sketch.
Certain systematic philosophical themes
emerge in Midgley's large corpus. And I
want to end by saying something very
briefly about two of these. Midri is
sometimes regarded as a philosopher of
science. Much of her work is directed
against a prevalent scientism, the idea
that anything that needs to be explained
can be explained using the tools of the
physical sciences. Such explanations
typically work by explaining large-scale
happenings in terms of occurrences that
take place at smaller scales and that
involve the entities that populate the
theories of those disciplines, various
forces, subatomic particles, genes, and
so on.
As such, the scientistic ideal is
reductive and homogenizing. It involves
explaining a whole by reducing it to its
parts. An at an atomistic vision of the
world. As I've said, Midley's synthetic
explanatory strategy involves
constellating the partial truths that
belong to different perspectives into a
whole. Far from being reductive, her
explanatory aim is to enlarge our
perspective. For this we need to draw on
insights from beyond the special
sciences from ethology, psychology and
the social sciences but also from
history and literature. And here from
Midi, poetry has a special role to
play. Just as our nature is comprised of
a natural set of motives that can be
reordered depending on what we value,
our collective lives together are
structured by narratives or myths. These
myths help to place us in the world and
they encode their own ordering of
values. Mind over matter, reason over
emotion, man over beast. Part of the
work of the philosopher, she thinks, is
to analyze the myths and narratives that
shape our lives. By revealing the
original need from which they sprung,
the philosopher can expose where they
are no longer useful. That is, where the
pattern of life they recommend dulls our
collective capacity to live as we can.
But to do this, Mary thinks the
philosopher also needs the skills of the
poet. Poetry has the capacity to refresh
our ideas and language, to repattern our
associations and convey imaginative
visions. The poet has in her store the
tools of metaphor and analogy. She can
spotlight unexpected relations or zoom
out and home in on some aspect of
synthetic truth, help us to see as
connected things that we had previously
not connected or which we had not even
noticed. This is effectively what Miji
is doing in Beast and Man. We are not
just like animals, we are animals, is
her opening line. The vision that she
communicates is to invite a repatterning
of our values. It is no coincidence that
Miji's words often delight. They
sometimes have an Edwardian ring, but
their cadence is also prophetic and
sometimes grave. She concludes that book
with these words, "Man can neither be
understood or saved
alone." In the 1980s, Miji led a
campaign to stop Newcastle University's
philosophy department from being closed
down. Her last book, what is philosophy
for, was written when she was 98, and
there is a sense in which all of her
work can be read as a plea for why
philosophy matters. It is notable, she
says, that Socrates did not speak of the
dangers of an unexam of unexamined
thought or unexamined language, but of
an unexamined life. Whenever there are
gaps in understanding, viewpoints that
seem intractably irreconcilable, where
there is conflict and confusion, uh
where our reason is dislocated, this is
where philosophy is needed. This is the
need philosophy answers because we are
creatures that value and need order,
integration, and sense. But to seek such
order is paradoxically to be liberated.
For midi, it is to find ourselves as
part of the whole in which we are at
home.
